title: Batting average (cricket)

infobox.value: In [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket"), a player's **batting average** is the total number of [runs](/wiki/Run_(cricket) "Run (cricket)") they have scored divided by the number of times they have been [out](/wiki/Dismissal_(cricket) "Dismissal (cricket)"), usually given to two decimal places. Since the number of runs a player scores and how often they get out are primarily measures of their own playing ability, and largely independent of their teammates, batting average is a good metric for an individual player's skill as a batter (although the practice of drawing comparisons between players on this basis is not without criticism[[1]](#cite_note-1)). The number is also simple to interpret intuitively. If all the batter's [innings](/wiki/Innings "Innings") were completed (i.e. they were out every innings), this is the [average](/wiki/Arithmetic_mean "Arithmetic mean") number of runs they score per innings. If they did not complete all their innings (i.e. some innings they finished [not out](/wiki/Not_out "Not out")), this number is an estimate of the unknown average number of runs they score per innings.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Cricket statistic

|  |
| --- |
| {\displaystyle \mathrm {Batting~average} ={\frac {\mathrm {Runs~scored} }{\mathrm {Number~of~times~out} }}} |

In [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket"), a player's **batting average** is the total number of [runs](/wiki/Run_(cricket) "Run (cricket)") they have scored divided by the number of times they have been [out](/wiki/Dismissal_(cricket) "Dismissal (cricket)"), usually given to two decimal places. Since the number of runs a player scores and how often they get out are primarily measures of their own playing ability, and largely independent of their teammates, batting average is a good metric for an individual player's skill as a batter (although the practice of drawing comparisons between players on this basis is not without criticism[[1]](#cite_note-1)). The number is also simple to interpret intuitively. If all the batter's [innings](/wiki/Innings "Innings") were completed (i.e. they were out every innings), this is the [average](/wiki/Arithmetic_mean "Arithmetic mean") number of runs they score per innings. If they did not complete all their innings (i.e. some innings they finished [not out](/wiki/Not_out "Not out")), this number is an estimate of the unknown average number of runs they score per innings.

Each player normally has several batting averages, with a different figure calculated for each [type of match](/wiki/Forms_of_cricket "Forms of cricket") they play (first-class, one-day, Test matches, List A, T20, etc.), and a player's batting averages may be calculated for individual seasons or series, or at particular [grounds](/wiki/Ground_(cricket) "Ground (cricket)"), or against particular opponents, or across their whole career.

Batting average has been used to gauge cricket players' relative skills since the 18th century.

Batting averages are sometimes calculated for whole teams, across a series or tournament.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Values

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Batting_average_(cricket)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Values")]

Most players have career batting averages in the range of 20 to 40. This is also the desirable range for [wicket-keepers](/wiki/Wicket-keeper "Wicket-keeper"), though some fall short and make up for it with keeping skill. Until a substantial increase in scores in the 21st century due to improved bats and smaller grounds among other factors, players who sustained an average above 50 through a career were considered exceptional, and before the development of the [heavy roller](/wiki/Heavy_roller "Heavy roller") in the 1870s (which allowed for a flatter, safer cricket pitch) an average of 25 was considered very good.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

* All-rounders who are more prominent [bowlers](/wiki/Bowler_(cricket) "Bowler (cricket)") than batsmen typically average something between 20 and 30.
* 15 and under is typical for specialist bowlers.
* A small number of players have averaged less than 5 for a complete career, though a player with such an average is a liability unless an exceptional bowler such as [Alf Valentine](/wiki/Alf_Valentine "Alf Valentine"), [B. S. Chandrasekhar](/wiki/B._S._Chandrasekhar "B. S. Chandrasekhar") or [Glenn McGrath](/wiki/Glenn_McGrath "Glenn McGrath") were.

Career records for batting average are usually subject to a minimum qualification of 20 [innings](/wiki/Innings "Innings") played or completed, in order to exclude batsmen who have not played enough games for their skill to be reliably assessed. Under this qualification, the highest [Test](/wiki/Test_cricket "Test cricket") batting average belongs to [Australia](/wiki/Australia_national_cricket_team "Australia national cricket team")'s Sir [Donald Bradman](/wiki/Donald_Bradman "Donald Bradman"), with 99.94. Given that a career batting average over 50 is exceptional, and that only [4 other players](/wiki/List_of_Test_cricket_records#Highest_career_batting_average "List of Test cricket records") have averages over 60, this is an outstanding statistic. The fact that Bradman's average is so far above that of *any* other cricketer has led several statisticians to argue that, statistically at least, he was the greatest athlete in any sport.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Disregarding this 20 innings qualification, the highest career Test batting average is 144 by [Kurtis Patterson](/wiki/Kurtis_Patterson "Kurtis Patterson"), who scored 144 runs and was dismissed once in his two Test innings. He then fell out of the Australian squad due to a loss of form and injury.

Batting averages in [One Day International](/wiki/One_Day_International "One Day International") (ODI) and [T20 International](/wiki/T20_International "T20 International") (T20I) cricket tend to be lower than in Test cricket because of the need to score runs more quickly. Consequently, batters tend to play riskier strokes and less emphasis is placed on building an innings in order to amass a high individual score. It should also be remembered, especially in relation to the ODI and T20I histograms above, that there were no ODI or T20I matches when Bradman played.

## Interpretation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Batting_average_(cricket)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Interpretation")]

If a batter has been dismissed in every single innings, then this statistic gives exactly the [average](/wiki/Arithmetic_mean "Arithmetic mean") number of runs they score per innings.

However, for a batter with one or more innings which finished [not out](/wiki/Not_out "Not out"), the true mean or average number of runs they score per innings is unknown as it is not known how many runs they would have scored if they could have completed all their not out innings. In this case, this statistic is an [estimate](/wiki/Estimation "Estimation") of the average number of runs they score per innings. If their scores have a [geometric distribution](/wiki/Geometric_distribution "Geometric distribution"), then this statistic is the [maximum likelihood estimate](/wiki/Maximum_likelihood_estimate "Maximum likelihood estimate") of their true unknown average.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Batting averages can be strongly affected by the number of not outs. For example, [Phil Tufnell](/wiki/Phil_Tufnell "Phil Tufnell"), who was noted for his poor batting,[[7]](#cite_note-7) has an apparently respectable [ODI](/wiki/One_Day_International "One Day International") average of 15 (from 20 games), despite a highest score of only 5 not out, as he scored an overall total of 15 runs from 10 innings, but was out only once.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

A batter who was not dismissed in any of the innings over which their average is being calculated does not have a batting average, as [division by zero](/wiki/Division_by_zero "Division by zero") does not give a result.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Leading male batting averages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Batting_average_(cricket)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Leading male batting averages")]

### First-class

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Batting_average_(cricket)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: First-class")]

### Test matches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Batting_average_(cricket)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Test matches")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Donald_Bradman_australian_cricket_player_pic.JPG/250px-Donald_Bradman_australian_cricket_player_pic.JPG)](/wiki/File:Donald_Bradman_australian_cricket_player_pic.JPG)

[Don Bradman](/wiki/Don_Bradman "Don Bradman")

See also: [Highest career batting averages in Tests](/wiki/List_of_Test_cricket_records#Highest_career_batting_average "List of Test cricket records")

A batting average of above 50 is considered by many as a benchmark to distinguish between a good and a great batsman.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Highest male career batting averages in [Test matches](/wiki/Test_cricket "Test cricket") as follows:

| 3 | [Australia](/wiki/Australia_national_cricket_team "Australia national cricket team") [Adam Voges](/wiki/Adam_Voges "Adam Voges") | 20 | 31 | 7 | 1,485 | 269\* | **61.87** | 2015–16 |

| 7 | [England](/wiki/England_cricket_team "England cricket team") [Eddie Paynter](/wiki/Eddie_Paynter "Eddie Paynter") | 20 | 31 | 5 | 1,540 | 243 | **59.23** | 1931–39 |

## Alternatives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Batting_average_(cricket)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Alternatives")]

Alternative measures of batting effectiveness have been developed, including:

### Strike rate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Batting_average_(cricket)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Strike rate")]

Main article: [Strike rate](/wiki/Strike_rate "Strike rate")

Strike rate measures a different concept to batting average – how quickly the batsman scores (i.e. average number of runs from 100 balls) – so it does not supplant the role of batting average. It is used particularly in [limited overs matches](/wiki/One-day_cricket "One-day cricket"), where the speed at which a batter scores is more important than it is in [first-class cricket](/wiki/First-class_cricket "First-class cricket"). Strike rate may also be used to compare a player's ability to score runs against differing types of bowling (i.e. spin, fast bowling).

### Player rankings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Batting_average_(cricket)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Player rankings")]

Main article: [ICC Player Rankings](/wiki/ICC_Player_Rankings "ICC Player Rankings")

A system of player rankings was developed to produce a better indication of players' current standings than is provided by comparing their averages.
